Short_Description: A tanker vessel's crew member was exposed to a toxic atmosphere while tank cleaning resulting in becoming unconscious and medevac'd by SAR.
Description: The crew of a tanker were carrying out tank cleaning operations after discharging natural gas condensate. The cargo tank had been gas freed and checked at each corner however when the crew started working in the tank, one of them became dizzy and disorientated. The other crew members raised the alarm, rescued the unwell crewmember from the tank and then administered oxygen after consultation with a doctor. The crew member was then evacuated ashore as a precaution. After medical treatment ashore they were able to return to the vessel at the next port. The reason for the crew member being exposed to the gas was because when checking the tank before cleaning, the crew had not considered the possibility of gas collecting in pockets in the tank. The vessel amended their risk assessments, carried out training and safety meetings and circulated the lessons learned amongst the fleet.
On_Duty: Yes - On duty prior to casualty

Short_Description: A utility vessel crewmember was injured when the vessel collided with a riser that they were working on.
Description: The crew of a utility vessel were working on a pipeline riser, connecting an anchor set. They sustained some ligament strains to their neck, head and right forearm and had suspected water inhalation therefore the hospital kept them in for observations. Short_Description: Utility vessel crew member fell overboard whilst servicing a buoy
Description: A utility vessel was alongside preparing to service a buoy, the vessel's workboat was sent to rig the buoy for recovery. The workboat approached the buoy and the recovery wire was passed to the forward deck to be secured into the main winch wire. As the crew member was securing the recovery pennant the weight came onto the pennant and overbalanced the crew member, resulting in them falling over the bow, onto the buoy and then into the water. The workboat recovered the crew member within one minute and bought back alongside. It was noticed the lifejacket had not inflated when it made contact with the water. The casualty was assessed, they had bruising and grazing to the thigh. They went below to warm up with another crew member, after a few minutes, the casualty went into shock and was unresponsive. The casualty was taken to hospital for further assessment and an x-ray.

Short_Description: A buoy laying vessel crewmember fell when dismounting from a crane and injured their leg.
Description: A buoy laying vessel crewmember was on top of a hatch on the main working deck inspecting the maintenance carried out on the speed crane. When dismounting the hatch, the crewmember used a nearby fender and the catface of the crane as a support. The catface was not secure and gave way when weight was applied to it. This caused the crewmember to fall and land awkwardly on a grappling hook.  The crewmember sustained a graze to their left shin with the leg starting to swell and bruise. The crewmember was assisted off the deck and removed their rigger boots. The first aid team issued cool pack for the swollen leg. No other pain medication was given. As the vessel was alongside the decision was made to have it examined at the hospital. After assessment at hospital, the crewmember returned home due to the severity of their injury.

Short_Description: A replenishment tanker experienced a laundry room fire due to an electrical fault on a tumble dryer
Description: On board a replenishment tanker a tumble dryer in the laundry room caught fire after an electrical fault occurred. Night watch crewmembers put on some laundry for the night when shortly after the fire detector was activated. The crew confirmed that there was a fire and conducted first aid fire fighting. The ship mustered at harbor emergency stations and the ventilation was crash stopped and the accommodation fire doors were closed. The fire was extinguished by the crew using portable fire extinguishers. There was no damage besides the tumble dryer was no longer operational due to minor fire damage. There were no serious injuries but one crew member experienced minor shock in the form of a panic attack and another crewmember in the secondary fire fighting party who went to check for re-ignition suffered minor breathing difficulties. The cause of the fire was investigated and identified as coming from the power supply cable that had been chafing against the the cowling resulting in the breakdown of the wire isolation.

Short_Description: Service vessel crew member was struck in the head by escape hatch.
Description: a crew member was assigned to check the emergency escapes in the machinery spaces, when they came to check the escape hatch in the converter room STBD side. At first, the crew member struggled to unlock the hatch via the wheel, once they did the hatch was also difficult to lift. As they were opening the hatch their footing slipped which caused them to concentrate on preventing myself from falling from the top of the ladder and not on the hatch itself. In the moment, the hatch dropped from midway open landing on the left side of their head. The incident was not witnessed by anyone as they were sent to check them on their own. Following the knock, then attempting to come down the ladder before realizing they had not locked it back off, they then returned up the ladder and that is when the pain and realization of what had just happened hit them. As a result of this incident, they had suffered concussion and a cut that is approximately 2 and a half inches long situated on the left side of the crew member forehead.

Description: A passenger on board a RIB (rigid inflatable boat) sustained a dislocated shoulder and was evacuated by search and rescue services to a shoreside hospital. The RIB was taking part in a training day and a student was at the helm with an instructor behind them. The nose of the vessel dropped into the trough of a wave causing spray and the vessel to decelerate rapidly. The injured crewmember was thrown from where they were sitting on the sponson and struck the deck. The crewmember stated that they knew right away that they had dislocated their shoulder and the vessel returned to port. However, due to the fact that the crewmember was in significant pain and their arm was numb, the crew of the RIB issued a PAN PAN and the crewmember was transferred to a search and rescue vessel and then to a shoreside ambulance. The crewmember was signed off for five days due to their injury.

Short_Description: A search and rescue vessel capsized whilst assisting a foundering fishing vessel
Description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked with assisting a fishing vessel which was taking on water. Once the SAR vessel was alongside, two crew were placed on the fishing vessel to aid with the pumping out the vessel, though these efforts proved ineffective with the vessel becoming unstable. The SAR crew decided that evacuation was required and the SAR vessel came alongside the casualty vessel as it began to capsize. The fishing vessels mast struck the midships of the SAR vessel preventing the helm from pulling away. The fishing vessel continued capsize with the mast pressing down, eventually causing the SAR vessel to capsize with two crew aboard. Following the capsize, in accordance with standard operating procedures, the crew exited the overturned hull and began the righting procedure. No injuries to the SAR crew or fishing vessel crew.

The injured person misjudged their hand placement and placed it into the still-rotating fans, which could not be stopped immediately, resulting in amputation of their ring finger.

Short_Description: A search and rescue vessel suffered a failure of directional control during a training exercise
Description: A search and rescue vessel was carrying out a training exercise involving the operation of the emergency steering system. The skipper turned the system over to the emergency system while travelling at high speed but due to disconnected communications, the other crew did not hear the warning. The torque of the propellers during the sudden maneuver caused the boat to swing to port and stop suddenly. One crewmember was thrown over board and another fell onto the floor of the boat. The vessel recovered control and was able to rescue the crewmember who fell over board. There were no serious injures, pollution or damages. The incident reportedly happened due to lack of communications and conducting the event at high speed rather than stopping to change over to emergency steering. The skipper of the vessel had practiced this at another rescue boat station without incident so didn't anticipate the outcome.
On_Duty: Yes - On duty prior to casualty

Short_Description: A oil tankers crew member had oil contaminate there eyes while taking a sample, resulting in being taken to hospital for check up.
Description: a crew member proceeded to take three samples of Marine Gas Oil (MGO). Under normal procedures, supplied sample bottles are used for this task but the receiving vessel requested that the sample be taken using their own sample bottle. The bottle provided was of a different design from the standard sample bottles. While sampling, the crew member attempted to collect the MGO using the unfamiliar bottle. As they did the MGO sprayed back unexpectedly and made contact with there face, including their eyes.  Immediate first aid actions were taken. The crew member was escorted directly to the onboard shower to wash the affected area, and the crew used the full supply of onboard saline solution to flush there eyes thoroughly.  The office was notified and arrangements were made for the crew member to be transported to A&E for further medical assessment and treatment.

Short_Description: A motor cruiser and pwc collided resulting in one injury and damage to both vessels.
Description: A motor cruiser and Jetski collided with the Jetski hitting the the starboard side of the motor cruiser near the bow. The Jetski and the master of the Jetski went underneath the motor cruiser. The master of the motor cruiser then stopped the engine of the motor cruiser. The Jetski master and Jetski then surfaced at the stern of the motor cruiser. The master and passengers in the motor cruiser retrieved Jetski master from the water. The Jetski master had multiple serious wounds, believed to be from the propeller of the motor cruiser. The motor cruiser then headed at speed towards a pier, with the passengers attracting the attention of the officials in the lake. The official called the emergency services and attended to the Jetski master until the ambulance arrived. The Jetski master was then taken to hospital due to the lacerations to his torso and upper leg as well as a head injury. The injuries were sufficiently significant that the master of the Jetski was taken to intensive care but the injuries were not life threatening.

Search and rescue (SAR) assets were deployed to assist the sailors, three sailors were recovered by the sailing club safety boat and the other two recovered by the SAR vessel. The three dinghies were recovered and brought ashore. The five sailors were assessed by paramedics and then discharged.

Short_Description: Pilot onboard a pilot vessel got winded by a handrail in a heavy swell whilst attempting an embarkation.
Description: A pilot onboard a cutter was going to embark a vessel in poor weather. The pilot made his way round the cutter on the outboard side of the boat. The sea and swell caused the pilot boat to become lodged against the ship’s belting resulting in a jolting motion. The pilot, who was holding the handrails tightly, was jolted into the rail resulting in him being “winded”. The boarding was aborted as it was deemed to unsafe to continue. The casualty was not seriously injured and returned to work that same day.

Short_Description: A passenger on an inland waterways passenger slipped and broke their leg.
Description: An inland waterways passenger was walking down the gangway, which was wet with rainwater, when they slipped. The group of people they were with, helped them to their feet and took them to hospital, where they received surgery for a complex fracture. The accident was reported by the passenger to the company that operates the vessel more than two weeks after the incident. Due to the passenger being helped by other passengers the incident was not reported directly to the crew at the time. The crew inspected the area for defects following the report. Crew who were on duty at the time of the accident stated that the passengers had consumed alcohol.

Short_Description: A ro-pax vessel crew member sustained a concussion after they were hit by a fridge door
Description: A ro -pax vessel crew member was working in the galley they had just finished using the upright fridge, they closed the door and turned around. The crew member felt a heavy impact to their head and realised that the fridge door had come off its hinges. Crew members administered first aid to the injured crew member, who was taken off duty and sent to rest with regular check-ups. A couple of days later the crew member was sent to hospital for an assessment and diagnosed with mild concussion.

Short_Description: Fishing vessel crew member sustained serious injuries during mooring operations
Description: A fishing vessel crew member was seriously injured during mooring operations when they stepped from the vessel to the quayside and slipped, resulting in them contacting the edge of the quay and hanging on. This resulted in broken ribs and a punctured lungs. The quay edge was broken with an angle towards the sea. The safety rail around the landing crane was loose. There was no damage to the vessel and no pollution and no other injuries beyond the aforementioned crewmember. There were no harbor staff on duty to assist in mooring operations present on the quayside at the time of arrival.

Short_Description: Fishing vessel crewmember sustained a head injury from a falling fish box during unloading operations.
Description: A fishing vessel crewmember was struck in the head by a falling fish box during unloading operations. The vessel was alongside and a crane was being used to remove the boxes from the fish room. The crane snagged and the operator moved the handle which caused the fish box to fall and striking a crewmember who was descending a ladder. The box hit the casualty in the head and they fell to the floor. The crew removed the casualty from the fish room to the deck. An ambulance was called but it took around 12 hours for the casualty to get to hospital. The casualty had a CT scan which was all clear and had stitching above the eye.

Short_Description: A ro-ro vessel shore worker sustained a broken leg during discharging operations
Description: A ro-ro vessel shore worker was struck by a forklift truck and sustained serious injuries. During discharging operations, a shore worker was driving a forklift down the loading ramp to discharge slate whilst four other shore workers were preparing ropes on the ramp in preparation for the loading of excavators. The injured shore worker was crouching down to move the ropes when the forklift driver struck them. They did not see the crouching shore worker and they did not hear the warning shouts of the other shore workers due to wearing ear defenders. The injured shore worker was taken to hospital and required surgery to repair the damage to their leg. The forklift worker was shaken by the incident. There were no damages or other injuries.

Description: A cruise ship passenger was exiting the restaurant, as they went through the entrance they tripped on the metal threshold and fell, landing on their knees and face. The passenger was taken to the medical centre where an x-ray showed the passenger had suffered a fracture to their neck. An additional x-ray the following day in port confirmed the fracture and the passenger was medically disembarked. The metal threshold at the restaurant's entrance was inspected and found without any deficiencies, with the frame in place between firm tiles.

Short_Description: A sailing yacht taking part in a race lost control of rigging injuring two crewmembers
Description: A recreational sailing vessel was taking part in a rally when they lost control of the rudder causing the boom and rigging to swing out of control. One crewmember was struck in the foot by the main sheet resulting in a compound fracture. Another crewmember was struck in the upper body by rigging resulting in a spinal injury, jaw fracture, shoulder fracture, tingling hands and feet and loss of consciousness. The sailing rig hydraulics were also damaged meaning that the sails could only be operated manually. Radio medical advice was sought to assist in stabilizing the injured crewmembers. Other vessels in the area and air assets responded to extract the two injured crewmembers to hospital.

Description: Injured Party (IP) climbed one flight of stairs coming from the car deck on the port side, having embarked as a foot passenger over the vessel vehicle deck. Whilst at the top on deck 3 the passenger tripped on the threshold of a door and fell onto the floor apparently scraping the back of both their hands. At the time no further injury was apparent but the IP was encouraged to attend a shoreside medical facility on arrival as their skin was very frail and the ships crew were worried about the cuts healing. Short_Description: Two powerboats collided during a training exercise
Description: Two powerboats were on a training session. Both vessels were in the same area with good distance between them whilst they were carrying out a planing exercise. One of the powerboats (1) began to slow down in preparation to swap over helms. In the process of slowing down, the other powerboat (2) whilst still planing was getting closer until both vessels were parallel. The helm in powerboat (1) reacted and turned to port, resulting in the two powerboats colliding with the bow of powerboat (2) riding up onto the sponson of the other powerboat. Both outboard engines were stopped. Two crew from powerboat (1) entered the water so prevent being hit by the bow. The two crew were wearing buoyancy aids and quickly recovered and transferred to another vessel in the area.

Short_Description: Cruise ship passenger fell overboard resulting in a fatality

A man overboard announcement was done by the Captain, the vessel also performed Search & Rescue Operations and the Security shipboard team locked down the scene. Captain called the local coastguard station and a rescue airplane was coming in approximately an hour for assistance as far as the ship cannot deploy a rescue boat due to adverse weather. At first light, two additional helicopters joined the search at the request of the local maritime coastguard station. A possible casualty was identified, the ship proceeded towards the area and one of the helicopters recovered the casualty, who was confirmed as deceased. which was the passenger who fell overboard.

Short_Description: A search and rescue vessel capsized after being caught by a wave
Description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on a routine training session near to shore before returning to base for a crew change.  The crew had set off and just before the incident they were parallel running within the surf zone.  A wave developed in front of the SAR vessel and the helm turned to run away/ parallel to it.  As the wave broke it was matching the speed of the vessel when the helm decided to turn and face the wave. The SAR vessel punched into the wave and was engulfed as it broke over the bow.  The bow started to climb until the boat was vertical and eventually capsized.  Two crew were thrown clear and one ended up in the air space between the vessel and water before they were helped by the crew.

Short_Description: Commercial yacht crew member missed footing falling down from a step onto cabin floor injuring head and resulting in a fatality
Description: Whilst going below for a moment prior to the mooring, the skipper missed his footing and fell from the second step onto the cabin floor. The fall resulted in an open head wound about 2-3 inches long and considerable lower back pain. His immediate treatment on board was under the care of a crew member while the vessel made for a marina. Once safely alongside, a request for paramedic assessment was made. An ambulance was dispatched and he was taken ashore and assessed in the ambulance. At this point, the decision was made to transfer him to the hospital, where his head wound was treated and he was kept overnight for observation. He sustained no fractures and was released after 12 hours as he had no fractures. It seemed he recovered well, the following day and he noticed his head wound was still tender he was advised to attend the hospital where he was admitted as his injury had developed into something infectious and needed surgery. He wasn't able to recover from this and passed away.  Crew member fall down a step into the cabin of a commercial yacht, resulting in a fatal head injury.

Short_Description: Yachts pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations.
Description: Yachts pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. It was the usual set-up that we’ve seen on previous occasions in that the boarding ladder was suspended from the deckhead and was not sitting flush against the vessels side due to belting arrangements. The vessel had a small shell, stores door also available for boarding however, large fenders were rigged either side by the crew and when asked to remove them, the pilot was told by the vessels Master that they wouldn’t as was worried about the paintwork. The Pilots assessed the situation and informed the vessel that unless the fenders were removed, then there would be no boarding undertaken. Fenders were removed and boarding was conducted through the shell door. It must be mentioned at this point that the entire operation was videoed from the bridge wing and by the crew making the pilots feel “intimidated and under pressure.” On boarding the duty pilot hit his head, sustaining a small injury (see attached) and at no point did the crew ask about his welfare or offer any first aid (they handed the pilot a pair of over shoes so as not to dirty their decks).
On_Duty: Yes - On duty prior to casualty

Short_Description: A fishing vessel crewmember sustained a crush injury.
Description: A fishing vessel crewmember was working on the net drum, operating the controls and guiding the net at the same time. They got their arm caught in the mechanism leading to a crush injury to their bicep and subsequent swelling. They were given first aid and pain relief on board and the crew sought radio medical advice. They advised that the crewmember could remain on board so long as they was seen within forty-eight hours and that the injury should be monitored for increased swelling. The crew of the vessel had half a day fishing left to complete so decided to continue and then return to port afterwards so the injured person could be seen ashore.

Short_Description: A cruise ship crew member suffered a cardiovascular compromise and fractured shinbone as a result of unexpected tension on a guide rope used to control mooring line placement onto winch drum
Description: During unmooring operations, a crew member was guiding the mooring line onto the winch after initial lines had been let go from the bollard and were being recovered.  The mooring lines were coiled on powered drums, one being the main storage drum and then tension is placed on the split drum. As the line was cast off and being recovered the crew member was using a small diameter guide rope to ensure that the line was layered evenly onto the main storage drum. An investigation is in progress as to the exact cause of injury, suspected to be as a result of unexpected tension on guide rope used to control mooring line placement onto winch drum.  The unmooring operation was suspended when the alarm was raised, and the crew member was disembarked for local medical attention. The crew member was diagnosed with a cardiovascular compromise to the right lower leg by strangulation injury and shinbone fracture.

Short_Description: A search and rescue vessel capsized whilst on service.
Description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to a casualty in the water who had been cut off by the incoming tide. The location of the casualty required the SAR vessel to veer down to achieve a suitable position to recover the casualty.  During this manoeuvre the anchor became dislodged allowing the vessel to turn around into the surf, and possibly the chain caught in the propeller leading to the loss of propulsion. As a result the vessel capsized on the next set of waves. The three crew swam away from the shoreline to deeper water where they were rescued by another SAR vessel. No injuries were sustained to the crew. The vessel was later recovered and there was some damage to the propeller.

Short_Description: Ro/Ro passenger crewmember injured their back whilst moving a wheeled pallet cage.
Description: A Ro/Ro passenger vessel received catering stores aboard the car deck on a wheeled pallet cage. Two crew were in the process of moving the stores from the car deck into the passenger lift. One crewmember at the front pulling it into the lift and the injured party at the back, pushing it. There was a lip on the deck at the doorway into the lift and the 2 crew were struggling to wheel the stores inside. While pushing and tilting the stores into the lift, the injured crewmember felt a sudden shooting pain in his back. He was just about to start his downtime and said he would rest it for a while and see if it improved, but after about an hour said it wasn't getting any better and didn't feel he could do his job properly any more. A relief was arranged and the crewmember went off the vessel.

Short_Description: Offshore crew transfer vessel crew member fractured ribs  after falling into a open void whilst washing windows
Description: The vessel had sailed to bring a welding team to a WPB pontoon and moored alongside the pontoon. The welding team was transferred and the vessel remained alongside and waited for the welding team to bring them back to the shore when they are finished. The crew opened a hatch cover to ventilate the void because condensation was observed in the void. Sometime later it was decided to clean the windows, two of the windows are located above the open void. One of the crew members fell into the open void while cleaning the windows and broke five ribs. The crew member climbed out of the void and walked to the aft of the vessel where they met the captain, the captain stabilized the crew member and then informed the MCC. The Captain unmoored and sailed back to the Marina. The vessel arrived at the marina where an ambulance was waiting, the ambulance left the marina and brought the casualty to the hospital. The crew member was kept in hospital for 1 night for observation and was released from hospital in the afternoon the next day and is now recovering at a family residence.

The vessel continued to burn, breaking free from its moorings, causing minor damage to two other moored boats before sinking. There was damage to the quay where the vessel was berthed. Two crewmembers had a leg injuries and another had foot burns. No one was severely injured or taken to hospital. One person was given first aid at the scene.
On_Duty: Yes - On duty prior to casualty

The other couplings were checked. The crewmember was signed off work for eight days.

Short_Description: A passenger on a RIB trip sustained a back injury
Description: A RIB was on a sight seeing trip with six passengers and the helm.  The passengers had received a safety briefing before departure, donned weatherproof clothing and lifejackets and commenced the trip. Whilst on passage the helm stopped twice to provide information to the passengers on holding the bar in front and using their legs to brace and to check passenger wellbeing. The RIB was travelling approximately six knots when the RIB encountered a large wave, the passengers braced as the RIB came off the the top of the wave and landed in the trough. A passenger raised their hand to alert the helm that they had hurt their lower back. The helm immediately returned to sheltered waters, moved the passenger back to seats in front of the console, before returning to shore. The passenger was able to leave the RIB and recommended to seek medical assistance.

Skipper was given the all clear and put the propeller back in gear, rigging was still caught in the propeller, and became tight trapping the injured person (IP) against the bulwark. Short_Description: A search and rescue vessel capsized whilst on service.
Description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to a casualty in the water who had been cut off by the incoming tide. The location of the casualty required the SAR vessel to veer down to achieve a suitable position to recover the casualty.  During this manoeuvre the anchor became dislodged allowing the vessel to turn around into the surf, and possibly the chain caught in the propeller leading to the loss of propulsion. As a result the vessel capsized on the next set of waves. The three crew swam away from the shoreline to deeper water where they were rescued by another SAR vessel. No injuries were sustained to the crew. The vessel was later recovered and there was some damage to the propeller.
